key_insight: In this pilot biomonitoring study, prostate cancer survivors in New Jersey showed measurable PFAS exposure in serum and tap water, with only modest links between the two sources.
clinical_relevance: The findings suggest environmental PFAS exposure could be a relevant, underrecognized factor in prostate cancer survivorship research, especially in more diverse populations.
method: Recruited 124 prostate cancer survivors from Rutgers Cancer Institute (active surveillance or 3–12 months post-treatment).
Collected serum and residential tap water samples and measured PFAS using mass spectrometry and modified EPA Method 537.
Summarized PFAS detection and concentrations, and compared levels by race and serum–water correlations.
evidence_signal: Among 124 enrolled participants, several PFAS were frequently detected in serum and tap water, and serum–tap water correlations were modest (r = 0.22–0.41).
watch_item: This was a pilot study with partial sample availability and limited sample size, so results are preliminary and not yet enough to infer causality or exposure sources.
paper_title: Per- and Polyfluoroalkyl Substances Exposure in New Jersey Prostate Cancer Survivors: A Pilot Biomonitoring Study
